MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs sensible ablations (word vs char-CNN, attention vs no-attention) and reports comparisons on the same WIT3 training/eval splits, so core baselines are present and related work is discussed. However, from the summary I see gaps that a theory-first reviewer flags: decoder analyses rely on predicted (not gold) tags which can bias comparisons, and there is no mention of stronger or more diverse probing baselines (e.g., causal interventions or additional probe types) nor of uncertainty estimates across seeds. These omissions prevent a fully comprehensive fairness/robustness assessment, but overall the comparative evaluation is solid with only minor omissions. | B(4): The paper compares its representations against sensible baselines (purely word-based embeddings and a majority baseline) and conducts multiple controlled contrasts (with/without attention, char-CNN vs word, encoder vs decoder, layer depth, and target-language effects) on the same WIT3/IWSLT data, so the comparisons are largely fair. It also transparently acknowledges limitations (e.g., using predicted tags for decoder analysis) and discusses related work adequately. However, it omits stronger or more recent probing baselines (e.g., other frozen-representation probing methods or alternative encoder architectures) and lacks hyperparameter/code details that would aid reproducibility, so the empirical comparisons are thorough but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs sensible ablations (word vs char-CNN, attention vs no-attention) and reports comparisons on the same WIT3 training/eval splits, so core baselines are present and related work is discussed. However, from the summary I see gaps that a theory-first reviewer flags: decoder analyses rely on predicted (not gold) tags which can bias comparisons, and there is no mention of stronger or more diverse probing baselines (e.g., causal interventions or additional probe types) nor of uncertainty estimates across seeds. These omissions prevent a fully comprehensive fairness/robustness assessment, but overall the comparative evaluation is solid with only minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its representations against sensible baselines (purely word-based embeddings and a majority baseline) and conducts multiple controlled contrasts (with/without attention, char-CNN vs word, encoder vs decoder, layer depth, and target-language effects) on the same WIT3/IWSLT data, so the comparisons are largely fair. It also transparently acknowledges limitations (e.g., using predicted tags for decoder analysis) and discusses related work adequately. However, it omits stronger or more recent probing baselines (e.g., other frozen-representation probing methods or alternative encoder architectures) and lacks hyperparameter/code details that would aid reproducibility, so the empirical comparisons are thorough but not fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is adequate, so the logical flow (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) appears clear. However presentation omissions weaken clarity: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables or notation are fully specified—these gaps hinder reproducibility and force the reader to guess dataset/tagging details. From a theory-first perspective, the manuscript communicates its aims and findings clearly but would benefit from more explicit methodological and annotation details to avoid ambiguity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the methods and experiments (clear intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion flow). The writing quality is described as good and related work coverage adequate, and the experimental setup (freeze representations, probe classifiers) is communicated clearly. Presentation gaps include missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, and the decoder analysis limitation (use of predicted tags) could have been highlighted more explicitly in experimental descriptions. These are minor presentation issues rather than fundamental clarity problems, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is adequate, so the logical flow (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) appears clear. However presentation omissions weaken clarity: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables or notation are fully specified—these gaps hinder reproducibility and force the reader to guess dataset/tagging details. From a theory-first perspective, the manuscript communicates its aims and findings clearly but would benefit from more explicit methodological and annotation details to avoid ambiguity.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the methods and experiments (clear intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion flow). The writing quality is described as good and related work coverage adequate, and the experimental setup (freeze representations, probe classifiers) is communicated clearly. Presentation gaps include missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, and the decoder analysis limitation (use of predicted tags) could have been highlighted more explicitly in experimental descriptions. These are minor presentation issues rather than fundamental clarity problems, so I rate it a 4.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles an important, community-relevant question and delivers useful empirical findings (layerwise/attention/char-CNN effects). I give a score of 4 because the study provides actionable insights and solid experiments, but its claims are weakened by reliance on a single TED dataset, lack of causal/theoretical analysis, and missing reproducibility details; these issues merit improvement before a top-tier acceptance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper studies an NLP/MT question clearly within top-conference scope and addresses the right audience by probing encoder/decoder representations for morphology across architectures and attention variants. However, from a theory-first reviewer perspective it relies heavily on probing-by-freezing without causal interventions or a theoretical model explaining why encoder/decoder behaviors differ, uses a single TED-talk corpus (WIT3) limiting claims of generality, and omits key reproducibility details (no hyperparameters, seeds, or mention of code) and relies on predicted tags for decoder analysis. These weaknesses make it a borderline fit: interesting and relevant but missing the theoretical/experimental rigor I expect for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it asks a core representational question about NMT that matters to the community and uses established probing methodology (freeze representations + train classifiers) and controlled ablations (attention vs no-attention, encoder vs decoder, layerwise depth) that yield actionable insights (e.g., value of char-CNN, attention effects). While the work is limited to TED-talk parallel data and uses predicted tags for some decoder analyses, the empirical focus, multilingual considerations (effects of source/target morphological richness and target identity), and evaluation via POS/morph tagging and BLEU align well with standards and audience expectations for a flagship conference.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important empirical question and provides useful, systematic comparisons (encoder/decoder, attention ablations, char-CNN) that will motivate follow-up work. I give a 4 because the study’s controlled probing and clear findings make a meaningful contribution likely to influence NMT architecture and evaluation, while noting Reviewer A’s valid concerns about limited mechanistic explanations, reproducibility detail, and dataset scope that temper claims of transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper studies an important empirical question about what NMT encoders/decoders learn about morphology, and provides useful systematic comparisons (attention vs no-attention, char-CNN vs word-based) on WIT3—so it will inform practitioners and motivate follow-ups. However, from my theory-first perspective the work lacks mechanistic or causal interventions (beyond freezing representations) and omits key reproducibility details (no hyperparameters, single dataset, decoder analyses rely on predicted tags), which limits how strongly the community should update beliefs. The findings are likely to spawn further empirical and modeling work but do not by themselves provide a principled explanation or broadly generalizable theory, so impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important empirical question—what NMT models learn about morphology—using robust probing methodology (frozen representations and classifiers) across encoder/decoder and attention ablations, which aligns with my emphasis on controlled probing and componentwise analysis. Its findings (encoder vs decoder asymmetry, benefits of char-CNN, and attention’s effect on decoder representations) are likely to change how researchers design and evaluate NMT architectures and encourage follow-up work on representation and attention design. The work opens clear new directions (multilingual and layerwise probing, decoder-focused analyses) and is likely to be built upon by those studying representation quality, making it a strong-impact contribution beyond a narrow niche.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses established probing methods and offers an empirical, practical study rather than a new formal method. The work meaningfully extends prior probing by comparing encoder vs decoder, isolating attention effects, examining char-CNNs, depth and cross-linguistic behavior, which is a non-trivial and useful contribution for MT practitioners. Given these points, a score of 4 reflects notable but not revolutionary novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a focused empirical investigation into what NMT encoders and decoders learn about morphology (including effects of attention and char-CNNs), which is a useful perspective but largely an incremental contribution over existing representation-probing work. The questions addressed (morphological content in encoder vs decoder, role of attention, and morphologically-informed architectures) are sensible but not a fundamentally new method or theory; the work is primarily empirical on TED-talk data without causal interventions or formal models that would elevate novelty. An expert would find some of the specific findings (e.g., attention degrading decoder representations) informative, but the contribution does not substantially depart from established probing paradigms or introduce new techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies established probing methodology (freeze representations and train classifiers) but extends it in several meaningful ways: it compares encoder vs decoder morphology learning, isolates the effect of attention, studies char-CNN vs word-based inputs, and examines cross-linguistic and depth effects. These componentwise and attention-centered analyses differentiate the work from prior probing studies and would be informative to practitioners designing MT systems. Given the summary, the contributions are a notable, non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new method, so I rate it as clearly novel but not radically transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper contributes a useful, multilayer empirical probe suite (encoder/decoder, attention ablation, char-CNN vs word, layer analysis) and that key weaknesses are missing reproducibility details (hyperparameters, seeds, code) and reliance on a single domain with predicted decoder tags. Reviewer A’s concerns about lack of causal interventions and theoretical framing justify downgrading impact, while Reviewer B’s view that the empirical work is otherwise solid prevents a harsher score. Balancing these points yields a middle rating reflecting useful empirical contribution but limited robustness and generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide useful descriptive probes (encoder vs decoder, attention ablation, char-CNN vs word) but from my theory-first perspective they lack causal interventions or a formal model explaining why morphology is encoded, and rely heavily on probing-by-freezing which is known to be weak evidence of functional use. Reproducibility is weak—no hyperparameters or seeds reported and code availability is not mentioned—and the empirical claims rest on a single domain (TED talks) with predicted rather than gold decoder tags, limiting generality. Some sensitivity (attention/no-attention) is present, but critical analyses (multiple datasets, seed variance, theoretical account or interventions) are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a solid, multilayer empirical probe suite (encoder vs decoder, char-CNN vs word-based, attention vs no-attention, layer-depth analysis) across multilingual TED corpora that supports its core claims—this aligns with my emphasis on componentwise and cross-lingual analysis. However, important reproducibility and robustness details are missing (no hyperparameters or code, decoder evaluation relies on predicted rather than gold tags, and dataset/tagger specifics are underspecified), which prevents full verification and slightly weakens the empirical completeness.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results are coherent and support the main claims (encoder vs decoder morphology, char-CNN benefits, attention effects) but note important methodological and reporting gaps—lack of causal interventions, reliance on predicted tags for decoder analysis, limited dataset generality, and missing hyperparameters/statistics. These weaknesses reduce confidence but do not fatally undermine the experimental findings, so a score slightly above midpoint (4) reflects solid contribution with notable but addressable limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides experimental evidence that encoders capture morphology better than decoders and that char-CNN helps, but the evaluation relies heavily on probing-by-freezing (training classifiers on frozen features), a methodology I view as correlational without causal interventions to show representations are functionally used. Important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters, seeds, code) and decoder analyses depend on predicted tags rather than gold annotations and a single TED-talk corpus, limiting generality. There is also no theoretical model or causal analysis explaining why attention degrades decoder representations, so while the empirical results are suggestive they are not yet fully convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a coherent empirical methodology (train NMT, freeze representations, train probe classifiers) that directly supports the main claims about encoder vs decoder morphology, benefits of char-CNNs, and the role of attention; multilingual experiments and layer/component analyses are appropriate for these claims. However, there are methodological gaps: decoder analyses rely on predicted (not gold) tags, hyperparameters and statistical significance tests are not reported, and some baselines/controls (e.g., random or ablated probes, more diverse baselines) appear missing. These issues are important but do not fatally undermine the central experimental findings, so the work is technically solid with minor to moderate weaknesses.

main_claims: Neural MT encoders learn substantial morphological information useful for POS and morphological tagging, while decoders learn substantially less about target morphology.
Character-based (char-CNN) representations yield better morphological/POS tagging accuracy than purely word-based representations.
The attention mechanism reduces the quality of decoder-learned target-word representations, and removing attention improves decoder representations while decreasing encoder representation quality.
Representation quality is affected by factors such as source/target morphological richness, depth of encoding layer, and target language identity.
_summary.methodology: Train sequence-to-sequence NMT models (with and without attention) on parallel TED talks (WIT3/IWSLT 2016), freeze trained encoder/decoder to extract per-word representations, and train separate classifiers on those features to predict POS and morphological tags (using both predicted and available gold annotations).
datasets: Predicted-tag annotations produced by off-the-shelf taggers (not specifically named)
limitations: For decoder analysis they used predicted tags due to lack of available parallel data with gold POS/morphology tags.
Decoder representations perform poorly for POS tagging (limitation in decoder learning of morphology).

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics with a PhD in theoretical machine learning and 10+ years reviewing for ACL/ICLR; experience building formal models of representation learning.
traits: Penalises empirical-only papers that lack formal or causal explanation: deducts points if no theoretical model or causal intervention is provided to explain observed phenomena.
Requires reproducibility rigor: penalises if experiments lack multiple random seeds or uncertainty estimates (e.g., confidence intervals) for key results.
Demands dataset diversity: deducts points if claims of generality rely only on a single domain/dataset without clear justification.

Skeptical of probing-by-freezing methodology: lowers score when conclusions rely mainly on training downstream classifiers on frozen features without interventions demonstrating that representations are functionally used by the MT model.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I judge work primarily on whether it advances mechanistic understanding with theory or causal evidence; comprehensive empirical sweeps are secondary unless tied to principled explanations.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Probing Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with extensive experience in multilingual MT and representation analysis; active contributor to probing methodology and shared tasks.
traits: Rewards thorough, multilingual empirical analysis: gives credit when experiments cover multiple language pairs and realistic datasets demonstrating cross-linguistic effects.
Favors careful probing studies: rewards use of frozen-representation classifiers and systematic ablations that reveal what models learn, treating such results as valuable scientific evidence.
Values practical implications for MT system design: grants points if findings suggest actionable architecture choices (e.g., char-CNN, attention modifications) with empirical validation.
Prefers experiments that examine model components (encoder vs decoder, depth, attention): increases score when the paper provides layerwise and componentwise analyses that are well-controlled.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize robust, well-controlled empirical investigations that yield interpretable insights about model behavior and that can guide practitioners in designing better systems.
